name: Albert Heath
abstract: Albert "Tootie" Heath (May 31, 1935 – April 3, 2024) was an American jazz hard bop drummer, the brother of tenor saxophonist Jimmy Heath and the double-bassist Percy Heath. With Stanley Cowell, the Heaths formed the Heath Brothers jazz band in 1975.

concerts.label: Jazz: The Personal Dimension: Herbie Hancock Sextet

about_quotes.speaker: Kenny Barron
about_quotes.evidence: It was a band with Tootie Heath, Bob Cunningham. ... I don't remember what year it was. ... You’re bringing in your colleague Ted Dunbar, Dave Williams, Tootie Heath, Richard Landrum, and then Sonny Morgan.
about_quotes.doc_title: Kenny Barron — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Tom McIntosh
about_quotes.evidence: I had put together a group that I knew would not stay together. I had Art Farmer, James Moody, and myself on the front line. I had Tootie Heath on the drums, Tommy Flanagan and Richard Davis on bass.
about_quotes.doc_title: Tom McIntosh — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: John Heard
about_quotes.evidence: I have a band in LA called The Elders. ... Tootie Heath is in it
about_quotes.doc_title: John Heard interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, Scottsdale, Arizona, March 4, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Harold Mabern
about_quotes.evidence: Cedar Walton, one of my dearest friends, called and said, “Brother Mabern, we’re down here at Birdland with the Jazztet, you know, Art Farmer, Benny Golson, Cedar Walton, Tootie Heath and probably Tommy Williams on bass... So I sat in... So then I got in the band
about_quotes.doc_title: Harold Mabern interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, March 31, 2010

about_quotes.speaker: Art Farmer
about_quotes.evidence: My favorite record is one that I made many, many years ago called Art. It was on the Argo label with Tommy Flanagan playing the piano, Albert Heath playing the drums, and a bass player named Tommy Williams
about_quotes.doc_title: Art Farmer — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Wilbur Ware
about_quotes.evidence: That was the date with Cedar Walton, Kenny Dorham, Tootie Heath, Kenny Walden, Tudy Heath ... They had Tootie Heath on drums, Marvin Stamm on trumpet, Clifford on tenor and myself on bass
about_quotes.doc_title: 68088_Wilbur B. Ware, Sr oral history interview
